Satisfied! Here's a possible aftermath of the review:For starters, the place is charming. Ya soon as you step inside, you'll be greeted by the warm and inviting atmosphere. The decor is a mix of classic and modernist elements, with comfortable seating and plenty of greenery. The lumen is dim and intimate, making it perfect for a date or special occasion. The service is top-notch, with gracious and attentive servers who are knowledgeable about the menu. The menu itself is a masterclass in modern American cuisine, with a focus on seasonal component and creative aromas combinations. There are plenty of options to choose from, including minimum plates, salads, entrees, and desserts. The portions are generous, so you'll definitely leave premonition satisfied. One of the standout dishes is the roasted beet salad, which is served with a tangy goat cheese, sweet pickled walnuts, and a light capers dressing. It's a perfect example of how the stoves manages to balance flavors and textures in a modes that's both interesting and delicious. Another highlight is the grilled original ribeye steak, which is cooked to perfection and served with a rich Demi-glace. The steak is tender, moneymaking, and full of flavor, and it's paired with a side of garlic applesauce potatoes that are creamy and indulgent. If you're looking for something a little more gutsy, the kitchen also offers a variety of seasonal specials that exhibit the culinary team's creativity. For example, during our visit, they were offering a pan-seared duck breast with a cherry polenta and wild rice braised. It was a unique and flavorful dish that was simmered flawlessly. Of cours, no meal is complete without dessert. The pastry menu is full of creative and mouthwatering options, such as a chocolate lava biscuit, a bourbon-glazed pecan biscuit, and a lemon lavender crème brûlée. Our favorite was the crème brûlée, which had a wholeheartedly caramelized sugar top and a creamy, citrusy filling.

It's like a flavor explosion in my mouth, but I'm not sure if it's from the sauce or the questionable meat.I mean, it's definitely tasty, but it's the kind of taste that makes you wonder what else is in there.Like, is that a hints of rat in there?I'm not saying it is, but I'm also not saying it's not.I mean, it's definitely not rat, right?But then why does it have that weird aftertaste?I'm not sure if I'm just being paranoid, but I can't shake the feeling that something's off.But at the same time, it's so good. Like, seriously, seriously good.Like, I'd-eat-it-every-day good.But then I'd probably die from unknown meat disease.So maybe it's best to just stick to one or two a year.As a treat.A treat that makes me question my life choices.But in a good way?I think?I mean, it's definitely a conversation starter."Hey, have you ever eaten a Peppi's sandwich?""Yeah, I have. It's...an experience.""Yeah, I know what you mean. It's like a flavor bomb, but also a mystery meat bomb.""Exactly! But in a good way?""I mean, it's definitely a memorable experience.""Definitely. I'll never forget the time I ate a Peppi's sandwich.""Same here. And also the subsequent trip to the doctor to get my stomach checked.""Ah, yes. The classic Peppi's follow-up appointment."But even with all the questionable ingredients, I still want more.Like, maybe a Peppi's sandwich every week.But then I'd definitely die.So maybe just one every few months.As a special treat.A treat that makes me question my life choices and my sanity.But hey, at least it's not boring.Peppi's sandwiches are definitely not boring.They're more like a thrill ride for your taste buds.A thrill ride that may or may not end in a trip to the hospital.But hey, that's all part of the fun, right?

I was first surprised by the grand entrance and then by the incredibly high ceilings and the vast amount of seating. This place can accommodate a lot of people and not make you feel claustrophobic. It also felt cleaner than the other Japanese steakhouse in the University area (read: Kabuto). It probably helped that it didn't have that strip mall feeling, too. nnNakato offers sushi and other appetizers, but c'mon, who are they kidding? People come to a Japanese steakhouse for the hibachi dinners and to be entertained while it's cooking. Of course, the salad with ginger dressing that is served beforehand always tastes so much better than when you buy ginger dressing and pour it on your own salad at home. Why is that? nnThe entertainment and ensuing food was good, as expected. Nothing out of the ordinary; you know what you're getting into when you go to a Japanese steakhouse and we weren't disappointed. I do recommend Nakato more than Kabuto, though. The ambiance is better and the place feels a bit cleaner.

Classic American Chinese food served fresh and hot with excellent service. I can't believe that I had never eaten here until I got a Groupon. I even suffered severe" Groupon Guilt" when the meal was over and the check came. To be completely honest I could not bring myself to use it on my first visit. The food had been too wonderful. The wait staff was too good. I ordered 3 entrees that day.. . figuring I would take home the leftovers. I had scallion chicken made with dark meat chicken (your choice) , fresh vegetables with tofu ( medium spicy) and chicken and broccoli. All were outstanding.. nThe atmosphere is hard to find anymore. Three large aquariums dominate the two levels of dining area. I chose a booth in the bar area that is right next to one of the tanks. It is soo relaxing to just watch the fish swim. You understand why Drs offices always had aquariums back in the 1900s. nnIf you are looking for a Chinese food "fix" this is the place. I am tired of the little dingy take-out joints that are  in every strip mall. This place is an escape to a more sophisticated era of Chinese restaurants in Charlotte. I also tried the lunch buffet and it was excellent. Enjoy.

So far, so good. nnOur waitress was really friendly, but not over the top and kept a check on us frequently. nnI had an Italian Mojito which I don't recommend, period.. . blech. nnWe got the Zucchini Friti for an appetizer which was perfect and the dipping sauce was delish and the best part of our whole meal! I got a salad with ranch dressing - it was fine.. . nothing special. I ordered the Carmela's Chicken and it was bland, almost dry and overall just lacking. I brought more home than I ate and reheated with some garlic sauce I had making it better than the original for lunch the next day. I wouldn't get it again. My husband got the steak and had fries that were better than the steak, which I thought was WAY undercooked, even for medium. Blech! nnI would give our waitress an extra. 5 half star or maybe even a star, but our food was definitely not over a two star this visit. nnGood thing we had a gift card. We left a nice tip for our waitress with the balance since we had no plans of returning in the near future.

The restoration was done with fantastic attention to detail and it is so beautiful, you don't know where to look next. In fact, you may get a stiff neck from looking at the stunning ceiling, sparkling chandilers and incredible detail. nnIf you have long legs, or prefer to have space when you sit, your best bet is to sit on the floor level. Balcony seats provide a great view, but there is a considerably smaller amount of leg room. This can make a difference in your level of enjoyment of a show. nnI've seen two broadway touring shows there and the quality of both was great. Sound quality and lighting were great and I was impressed with the sets and staging that could be done on touring productions. nnMany times in older theaters, bathroom lines are an issue, but the layout and amount of bathrooms on the lower level of the theater make it less of an issue than it is at similiar locations. And there is even a bar on that level. nnThe few downsides to the theater are the high prices for drinks (but that is expected at any location like this) and the lobby gets really cold on winter days from all the patrons coming in and out. Theater doors usually open around a half hour before curtain time, so take that into consideration for your arrival time.

So I was there with a friend enjoying their fantastic potato salad and she had scarfed hers down in like 5 seconds and her fork was headed towards mine. I stabbed my fork in the same potato she had settled on and yelled NO! And just think, this is JUST the potato salad from here! nnThe burgers are the juiciest biggest most incredible burgers ever! I stick with the cheeseburger because I want to enjoy the burger without any fancy smancy stuff on it. I literally had the first bite of the burger and the juice squirted out the other side. Ok, I know that sounds dirty.. . but it was SO good!! I haven't strayed much from the burgers, but I did have the tuna melt once and it was good, but nothing compares to the burgers. I mean, that's what you go there for! Plus, my friend saw Russell Crowe there while he was filming a movie in the burgh, so you have to know it's good if his peeps recommended he go there!

**Continued review**:I am not sure what took me so long to actually leave a review, I guess I have just been enjoying the experience too much to get around to it. However, considering the amount of money I've poured into this place, I figured it was time to finally give them some credit. I think I may have a slight problem... I was first introduced to Pooley's when my buddy drug me along to check out the pinball machines they had in the back. The first night I went, the place was pretty dead with only a few people here and there scattered about. However, on my second visit, it was SLAMMED. The atmosphere was wild and crazy and it was a blast being surrounded by so much energy. Since then I have been back countless times with friends, with family, and even by myself (no shame).One thing that I love about this place, besides the awesome pinball machines (of course), is that there is no cover charge to get in. However, beware, once you start playing the machines, you will be hard pressed to stop. I have literally lost track of time on more than one occasion while playing there. The bartenders are awesome and super attentive (thanks, Stephanie!), the food is great (get the wings), and the beer selection, while not huge, has a great variety that changes often (I always find something good on tap). If you're into pinball, or are just looking for a place to drink some beers and chill with friends, Pooley's is the spot for you. In all, Pooley's is the perfect blend of a classic dive bar and a modern arcade. So, whether you're looking to get your pinball fix or just want to relax with friends, this is the place for you.

nnPersonally, I love this bar. This place is 5 miles from my office and 5 miles from my house, no wonder I made it to 200 beers so easily! nnThe Flying Saucer literally has approximately 300 unique beers, with at least 100 on tap, and some of them are rotating beers. Additionally, they have daily specials, like pint night with $3 pints and biggie beers for the price of a pint and even beer poker. Their loyalty program is also rad, drink 200 unique beers and get a $100 tab on the house to have a party with your friends (I'm 421). Fear not non-beer drinkers, they also have liquor drinks. nnTheir food menu is decent, I mean, it's bar food.. . The pretzels with queso dip are the best! And the scenery, aka the waitresses, aren't bad to look at either.. . in their school girl uniforms. They have pool tables in the back and good music streaming, unless a crappy band happens to be playing. If a band is playing and there is a cover, Beer Knurd members get in for free. nnProst!

The K (Big, Super or Regular) is the place where retail dreams go to die. It's a graveyard for aspirations, a wasteland for creativity, a soul-crushing abyss for anyone daring to dream beyond the mundane. The fluorescent lights flicker like a bad omen, casting an unflattering glow on the drab, uninspired merchandise. The aisles stretch out like a never-ending gauntlet, each one a reminder that innovation and originality are merely distant memories in this bleak retail landscape. The shelves are stocked with generic brands, their labels a sad reminder of the lack of care and attention put into each product. The employees drone on, their voices a monotone symphony of apathy, their eyesbereft of passion or enthusiasm. The dressing rooms are cramped and poorly lit, a metaphor for the limitations and constraints of the K's offerings. The checkout lines are an endurance test, a slow and agonizing journey towards the inevitable disappointment of leaving the store empty-handed. Even the building itself seems to be a relic of a bygone era, its architecture a sad reminder of a time when mediocrity was not only accepted but celebrated. In short, the K (Big, Super or Regular) is a place where hope goes to die, a bleak and joyless experience that serves only to remind us of the futility of human creativity and innovation.

nnTrying to count the total number of times I've been into the Harmony is futile; Yelp shows I have 16 check-ins, but I'd venture a guess that I check-in maybe once for every 2-3 visits. nnInteracting with the bartenders, one would think each of my visits was my first. They don't seem to recall regulars, or if they do, they don't seem to care much. Not looking for any special treatment, but when I walk-in, sit down and say, "Hi, how's it going? " I'd hope for a bit of a reply other than a grunt or a "I'm so busy I couldn't possibly bother with a response" eye roll. nnFood is continuing to slide; had a cheeseburger with fried onions last night.. . the dry bun overwhelmed the small, overcooked burger. A mound of fried onions delivered almost no extra flavor. Removed the bun and ate the burger solo. nnLake Louie Arena Premium is usually a fave of mine, but the one I had on Saturday (a few days earlier) tasted off - like the tap line was in need of a good cleaning. nnI also wasn't allowed to start a tab; it was pay-as-I-go.. . so, when I ordered food and a beer, I had to pay (and tip). Need a refill? Pay again. Ugh. nnThere are so many great places in the area; I'll be visiting the other haunts. So sad. nnPub chips, though.. . yum.

Absolutely love it. As I'm sure everyone who has been here has mentioned, it has a fantastic drink menu that offers a decent variety in terms of its alcoholic beverages - everything from various delicious martini-style drinks to the standard favorite beers. The place also features a really good menu; I'm very partial to its crab cake sandwich, which really hits the spot and has a perfect balance of crabcake with toppings and dressing. I especially love how any sauces or dips with appetizers are usually served in a martini glass. It's a cute and well-thought touch. nnThe only drawback here is that Olive or Twist is pretty darn popular; this place gets very noisy after 4: 00 very quickly, especially as the happy hour prices kick in. This is even assuming you can get in - I've tried to go here on nights when it's completely booked. Completely. Every table, top and bottom floor, with only standing room near the bar. nnI will continue to be a major fan because the drinks and eats really are fantastic, but will probably avoid coming here during its busiest days and hours (i.e. the end of the week and beginning of the weekend).

I hadn't been here in years and was kind of amazed how much it resembled pre-fire Ri Ra's. And yup, something about the atmosphere here (and in most other Pubs) puts me immediately at ease. nnThe game seemed to have them a bit frazzled, but you could also tell they were trying pretty hard. There isn't a TV for every 10 degree turn of the head, so that's a plus. Food is more interesting on the weekends, as they offer Irish brunch dishes - my Irish Benedict (1st try having gotten consumed by a table pal after arriving with rashers, a thick, ham-like meat) was good, with the potato cakes stealing the show. I'd come back on a non-World Cup weekend for this dish, absolutely. nnBloody Mary was meh, but it had a huge pickle spear as a garnish. Hard to be angry at a drink that comes with a pickle. nnFor me, Ri Ra's is a better place to hit off-hours with friends, all of us squished into a booth, talking about Ulysses. But its' sports bar alter-ego is definitively what pays the bills.

College students/active military pay $10. Wednesday is 1/2 price for ladies (but only after they pay full price on the first visit). Wednesday is also 1/2 price for guys who wear a sports bra. Yup, you heard me right. Wednesdays are usually pretty crowded. nn-They run a different membership special every month. I think the portions are pretty standard and they have a pretty goоd selection of drinks. I also don't like that they automatically adԁ gratuity to your bilⅼ if you have a party of 6 or more; it doesn't subject whether or not the service was bad, you're still going to pay 18% more. The best part about this place is the food, everything is made from scrath so it's fresh and delicious. Mana favorites are the bruschetta and the chicken parmesan. The priceѕ are reasonable, and you can get a good meal for around $20. The atmosphere is fortuitous and relaxed. It's a ɡreat place to go with friends or family. Overall, I really enjy this restaurant. The food is great, the prices are reasoable, and the atmosphere is relaxed. I would definitely recommend it to everyone looking for a good Italian restaurant.

My parents saw a recommendation to visit this place from Rick Sebak's "25 Things I Like About Pittsburgh" and he's usually pretty accurate. His recommendations were to try the Reuben, Fish Sandwich and Open-Faced Steak Sandwich. We went early afternoon for a late lunch today (a Saturday) and were seated right away. The staff is extremely friendly. My Mom & I each had the fish sandwich, while my Dad & Brother had a Reuben sandwich. The fish was very good, but the Reuben was to die for! Both dishes were massive, and could very easily be shared between two people. On top of being extremely large portions, it was incredibly affordable. The giant fish sandwich was $8 and the giant Reuben was $7. 50. Our drinks were always filled and we were checked on several times during the meal. We will definitely be back!! ! Oh and a bit of advice ahead of time - they take CASH ONLY. So come prepared, but I'm pretty sure I saw an ATM there as well. And I do believe they are closed on Sundays & Mondays.
